subject: maths
title: Permutations and Combinations
book: NCERT Class 11 maths — Permutations and Combinations

nodes.title: Apply the fundamental principle of counting (multiplication principle)
nodes.brief: Count the ways a sequence of independent choices can be made by multiplying the number of ways for each choice.
nodes.quote: If an event can occur in m different ways, following which another event can occur in n different ways, then the total number of occurrence of the events in the given order is
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The multiplication principle.
If one event can occur in m ways and, following it, a second event can occur in n ways, then the two events together (in that order) can occur in m x n ways. Example: with 3 pants and 2 shirts there are 3 x 2 = 6 outfit choices.

PART 2 — Extending to more than two events.
The rule generalises: for three successive events occurring in m, n, and p ways the total is m x n x p, and so on for any finite number of events. Example: 2 bags, 3 tiffin boxes, 2 bottles give 2 x 3 x 2 = 12 combinations for carrying one of each.

PART 3 — Repetition not allowed reduces each step's count.
When objects cannot be reused, the count for each successive choice shrinks by one. Example: arranging 3 letters from {A, B, C, D} without repetition gives 4 x 3 x 2 = 24 arrangements — the first place has 4 choices, the second only 3 (one used), the third only 2.
keyMoves: Break the task into a sequence of successive choices (events)
Count the number of ways each choice can be made in the chosen order
Multiply those counts together (m x n x p ...) to get the total
misconceptions: Adding the counts (m + n) instead of multiplying them
Keeping the count fixed when repetition is not allowed, instead of reducing it at each step

nodes.title: Recognise a permutation as an ordered arrangement
nodes.brief: Identify when a counting problem is a permutation: the objects are arranged in a definite order, so the order of writing them matters.
nodes.quote: A permutation is an arrangement in a definite order of a number of objects taken some or all at a time.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What makes it a permutation.
A permutation is an arrangement of objects in a definite order. The defining feature is that order matters: ROSE and ROES are two different permutations of the same four letters, because the sequence of letters differs. Whenever rearranging the same objects into a different sequence produces a new outcome you need to count, you have a permutation.

PART 2 — All at a time vs r at a time.
Permutations of n objects taken all at a time place every object into a sequence. Permutations of n objects taken r at a time select and arrange only r of the n objects. Example: 3-letter words from {A, B, C, D} are permutations of 4 objects taken 3 at a time; each word is a distinct ordered arrangement.

PART 3 — Permutation vs combination test.
Ask: does swapping the position of two chosen objects produce a new outcome? If YES, it is a permutation. The arrangement AB is different from BA in a permutation; in a combination (selection) they would be the same. Apply this test before choosing any formula.
keyMoves: Ask whether changing the order of the chosen objects gives a different outcome
If yes, treat the problem as counting permutations (ordered arrangements)
Distinguish 'taken all at a time' from 'taken r at a time' arrangements
misconceptions: Treating arrangements like AB and BA as the same when order actually matters
Confusing an arrangement (order matters) with a selection (order does not)

nodes.title: Evaluate and simplify factorial expressions
nodes.brief: Use n! as the product of the first n natural numbers, the convention 0! = 1, and the recursion n! = n(n-1)! to evaluate and cancel factorials.
nodes.quote: The notation n! represents the product of first n natural numbers
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition and basic values.
n! = 1 x 2 x 3 x ... x n, the product of the first n natural numbers. So 1! = 1, 2! = 2, 3! = 6, 4! = 24, 5! = 120, 6! = 720, 7! = 5040. By convention 0! = 1 (not 0).

PART 2 — Recursive peeling.
Every factorial can be peeled: n! = n(n-1)! = n(n-1)(n-2)! and so on. This exposes common blocks for cancellation. Example: 7!/5! = (7 x 6 x 5!)/5! = 7 x 6 = 42. Similarly 10!/8! = 10 x 9 = 90.

PART 3 — Evaluating nCr and nPr by cancellation.
Ratios of factorials simplify by cancelling the smaller factorial. Example: 12!/(10! x 2!) = (12 x 11 x 10!)/(10! x 2) = (12 x 11)/2 = 66. This cancellation step is the workhorse for every nPr and nCr calculation in this chapter.
keyMoves: Expand n! = 1 x 2 x ... x n, and use 0! = 1
Rewrite n! = n(n-1)! to expose a common factorial block
Cancel the common block to simplify a ratio of factorials
misconceptions: Taking 0! = 0 instead of 0! = 1
Treating (a+b)! as a! + b! or a!b! instead of leaving factorials of sums unsimplified

nodes.title: Count permutations of distinct objects with nPr = n!/(n-r)!
nodes.brief: Use the formula nPr = n!/(n-r)! for the number of ordered arrangements of n distinct objects taken r at a time (no repetition).
nodes.quote: This is a much more convenient expression for
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Deriving nPr from the multiplication principle.
Fill r ordered positions from n distinct objects (no repetition): the 1st position has n choices, the 2nd has (n-1), ..., the rth has (n-r+1). By the multiplication principle nPr = n(n-1)(n-2)...(n-r+1). Multiplying top and bottom by (n-r)! gives nPr = n!/(n-r)!.

PART 2 — Special cases and verification.
nP0 = n!/n! = 1 (one way to arrange nothing). nPn = n!/0! = n! (arrange all n objects). Example: 3-letter words from 6 distinct letters (NUMBER) number 6P3 = 6!/3! = (720)/(6) = 120.

PART 3 — Applying nPr to selection-and-arrange problems.
For JEE, common forms: chairman and vice-chairman from 12 people (order matters) = 12P2 = 12 x 11 = 132. Students on a podium from 10 = 10P3 = 10 x 9 x 8 = 720. Identify n (pool size) and r (how many seats), then apply the formula.
keyMoves: Identify n (total distinct objects) and r (number of ordered places)
Apply nPr = n!/(n-r)!, equivalently n(n-1)...(n-r+1)
Check special cases: nP0 = 1 and nPn = n!
misconceptions: Using nPr when order does not matter (it is then a combination)
Writing n!/r! instead of n!/(n-r)! for nPr

nodes.title: Count permutations when repetition is allowed (n^r)
nodes.brief: Use n^r for the number of ordered arrangements of n distinct objects taken r at a time when each object may be reused.
nodes.quote: The number of permutations of *n* different objects taken *r* at a time, where repetition is allowed, is
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Why the count stays constant at n.
When repetition is allowed, each position can independently be filled with any of the n objects — using an object does not remove it from the pool. So every one of the r positions has exactly n choices, regardless of what was placed before it.

PART 2 — Applying the multiplication principle to get n^r.
By the multiplication principle the total number of ordered arrangements is n x n x ... x n (r times) = n^r. Example: 4-letter strings from {R, O, S, E} with repetition = 4^4 = 256. Without repetition that would be 4P4 = 4! = 24, far fewer.

PART 3 — Contrast with no-repetition case.
3-letter words from the 6 letters of NUMBER: with repetition = 6^3 = 216; without repetition = 6P3 = 120. The repetition case always gives a larger (or equal) count. When a problem says 'digits can repeat' or 'letters can be reused', use n^r, not nPr.
keyMoves: Check whether each object may be reused (repetition allowed)
If so, give every one of the r places n choices
Multiply to get n^r (not nPr)
misconceptions: Using nPr = n!/(n-r)! even though repetition is allowed
Writing r^n instead of n^r

nodes.title: Count permutations when some objects are identical (n!/(p1!p2!...pk!))
nodes.brief: Divide n! by the factorials of the repeat counts to count arrangements of n objects in which some objects are indistinguishable.
nodes.quote: the letters of the word are not all different
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Why n! overcounts.
When some objects are identical, n! counts all arrangements as if they were distinct. Permuting the identical copies among themselves gives the same visible arrangement, so n! overcounts. If p objects of one kind are identical, the p! rearrangements of those copies all look the same, so we divide by p!.

PART 2 — General formula n!/(p1! p2! ... pk!).
If there are p1 objects of type 1, p2 of type 2, ..., pk of type k (totalling n), the number of distinct arrangements is n!/(p1! p2! ... pk!). Example: ALLAHABAD has 9 letters (4 A, 2 L, 1 H, 1 B, 1 D): 9!/(4! 2! 1! 1! 1!) = 362880/(24 x 2) = 362880/48 = 7560.

PART 3 — Worked example with constraints.
INDEPENDENCE has 12 letters: 3 N, 4 E, 2 D, 1 I, 1 P, 1 C. Distinct arrangements = 12!/(3! 4! 2!) = 479001600/(6 x 24 x 2) = 479001600/288 = 1663200. For a constraint like 'all 4 E together', treat EEEE as a single block: 9 items (block + 8 others) with repeats 3 N, 2 D gives 9!/(3! 2!) = 362880/12 = 30240.
keyMoves: Count n and the repeat count of each kind of identical object
Apply n!/(p1! p2! ... pk!), dividing out each group's internal arrangements
Combine with the multiplication principle for added constraints (fixed positions, blocks)
misconceptions: Using n! and forgetting to divide by the factorials of the repeated letters
Dividing by the number of repeats p instead of by p!

nodes.title: Recognise a combination as an unordered selection
nodes.brief: Identify when a counting problem is a combination: objects are selected without regard to order, so AB and BA are the same.
nodes.quote: In a combination, the order is not important.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — What makes it a combination.
A combination is a selection in which order does not matter. The selection {X, Y} is the same as {Y, X} — you do not arrange them, you merely choose them. Team selection, handshakes, chord-drawing, committee formation are all combination problems.

PART 2 — Concrete contrast with permutation.
From {A, B, C} taken 2 at a time: permutations are AB, BA, AC, CA, BC, CB = 6 (3P2). Combinations are {A,B}, {A,C}, {B,C} = 3 (3C2). Each combination gives rise to 2! = 2 permutations, so 6 = 3 x 2. This factor of r! is the bridge between the two counts.

PART 3 — Applying the order test.
Handshakes: if X shakes Y's hand, that is the same event as Y shaking X's hand. So handshakes among n people = nC2, not nP2. Lines through n points (no 3 collinear) = nC2 (a line through P and Q is the same as through Q and P). Use the test every time: does swapping two chosen objects make a new outcome? No -> combination.
keyMoves: Ask whether reordering the chosen objects gives a genuinely new outcome
If reordering does not matter (teams, handshakes, chords, committees), it is a combination
Avoid double-counting selections that differ only in order
misconceptions: Counting AB and BA as two outcomes when order is irrelevant
Treating a selection (combination) as if it were an arrangement (permutation)

nodes.title: Relate permutations and combinations by nPr = nCr x r!
nodes.brief: Use the fact that each combination of r objects yields r! permutations to connect nPr and nCr.
nodes.quote: There are as many as 6 combinations of 4 different objects taken 2 at a time
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — How combinations produce permutations.
Every unordered selection (combination) of r objects can be arranged in r! different orders, each giving a distinct permutation. So the total number of permutations equals the number of combinations multiplied by r!: nPr = nCr x r!.

PART 2 — Verification with small numbers.
4 objects taken 2 at a time: nC2 = 4C2 = 6 combinations ({AB,AC,AD,BC,BD,CD}). Each pair can be ordered in 2! = 2 ways. So permutations = 6 x 2 = 12 = 4P2 = 4x3. Check: 4P2 = 12. Confirmed.

PART 3 — Rearranging to get nCr.
From nPr = nCr x r! we get nCr = nPr/r! = [n!/(n-r)!]/r! = n!/(r!(n-r)!). This derivation is the cleanest way to remember the combination formula: start from permutations, divide out the internal orderings.
keyMoves: Note each combination of r objects gives r! ordered arrangements
Write nPr = nCr x r!
Rearrange to nCr = nPr / r! when a count of selections is needed
misconceptions: Forgetting the r! factor and equating nPr with nCr
Multiplying by r instead of r! when going from combinations to permutations

nodes.title: Count combinations with nCr = n!/(r!(n-r)!)
nodes.brief: Use nCr = n!/(r!(n-r)!) to count the number of unordered selections of r objects from n, including nC0 = nCn = 1.
nodes.quote: Now, we obtain the formula for finding the number of combinations of *n* different objects taken *r* at a time, denoted by
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The formula and boundary cases.
nCr = n!/(r!(n-r)!) counts unordered selections of r objects from n distinct objects, valid for 0 <= r <= n. Boundary: nC0 = 1 (one way to select nothing) and nCn = 1 (one way to select all). Example: 5C3 = 5!/(3! x 2!) = 120/(6 x 2) = 10 ways to choose a 3-person committee from 5.

PART 2 — Larger examples with factorial cancellation.
52C4 = 52!/(4! x 48!) = (52 x 51 x 50 x 49)/(4 x 3 x 2 x 1) = 6497400/24 = 270725 ways to pick 4 cards from 52. Always cancel the larger factorial block from the numerator to keep arithmetic manageable.

PART 3 — Combining nCr with multiplication and addition principles.
For constraints, split into cases and multiply nCr factors for joint independent choices. Example: a committee of 3 — 1 man from 4 men AND 2 women from 3 women = 4C1 x 3C2 = 4 x 3 = 12. For 'at least 1 woman', add across cases (0 men, 1 man, ...) or use complement: total - all-male.
keyMoves: Apply nCr = n!/(r!(n-r)!) for an unordered selection of r from n
Use nC0 = nCn = 1 for the boundary cases
For constraints, multiply nCr factors for joint choices and add over disjoint cases
misconceptions: Using nPr (order matters) where a selection nCr is required
Dropping the r! in the denominator and writing n!/(n-r)! for a combination

nodes.title: Use the symmetry nCr = nC(n-r)
nodes.brief: Apply nC(n-r) = nCr and the consequence that nCa = nCb implies a = b or a + b = n.
nodes.quote: selecting r objects out of n objects is same as rejecting (n - r) objects.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Why nCr = nC(n-r).
Choosing r objects from n is identical to choosing which (n-r) objects to leave out. Formally: nC(n-r) = n!/((n-r)! r!) = nCr. This symmetry simplifies arithmetic: instead of 20C17, compute 20C3 = 20 x 19 x 18/(3 x 2 x 1) = 6840/6 = 1140.

PART 2 — Solving nCa = nCb for unknowns.
If nCa = nCb then either a = b (trivial) or a = n - b, i.e. a + b = n. Example: if nC9 = nC8, then since 9 != 8, we must have n = 9 + 8 = 17. Then nC17 = 17C17 = 1.

PART 3 — Using symmetry to simplify multi-step problems.
In a problem asking for nC(n-2) when n = 10: use symmetry to get 10C2 = 45 directly rather than computing 10C8 the long way. Always replace the larger subscript with the smaller one when the subscript exceeds n/2.
keyMoves: Replace nCr by nC(n-r) (or vice versa) to use the easier subscript
From nCa = nCb, conclude a = b or a + b = n
Solve for the unknown n or r using that conclusion
misconceptions: Concluding only a = b from nCa = nCb and missing the a + b = n solution
Believing rejecting (n-r) objects gives a different count from selecting r

nodes.title: Apply Pascal's rule nCr + nC(r-1) = (n+1)Cr
nodes.brief: Use the identity nCr + nC(r-1) = (n+1)Cr that combines two adjacent combinations into one of the next row.
nodes.quote: selecting r objects out of n objects is same as rejecting
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Statement and intuition.
nCr + nC(r-1) = (n+1)Cr. Intuition: to choose r objects from (n+1) objects, either the (n+1)th object is included (leaving r-1 to choose from n, giving nC(r-1)) or excluded (choose all r from n, giving nCr). Adding these two disjoint cases gives (n+1)Cr.

PART 2 — Algebraic verification.
nCr + nC(r-1) = n!/(r!(n-r)!) + n!/((r-1)!(n-r+1)!). Factor out n!/((r-1)!(n-r)!) to get [1/r + 1/(n-r+1)] = (n+1)/(r(n-r+1)). So the total = n!(n+1)/(r!(n-r+1)!) = (n+1)!/(r!(n+1-r)!) = (n+1)Cr.

PART 3 — Pascal's triangle and applications.
This rule generates each entry of Pascal's triangle from the two entries above it. Row n of Pascal's triangle is nC0, nC1, ..., nCn. Applications: computing nCr from simpler values, proving identities, and understanding binomial coefficients — (a+b)^n = sum of nCr a^(n-r) b^r — which uses this structure.
keyMoves: Recognise two combinations with the same top n and subscripts r and r-1
Add them using nCr + nC(r-1) = (n+1)Cr
Verify by reducing both sides over a common factorial denominator
misconceptions: Writing nCr + nC(r-1) = nCr or (n+1)C(r-1) with the wrong top/subscript
Adding the combinations as if the formula were a simple sum of fractions without combining over a common denominator

nodes.title: Solve counting problems by choosing arrangement vs selection and combining the rules
nodes.brief: Model any counting problem by deciding whether order matters (permutation) or not (combination), then combine nPr, n^r, n!/(p1!...), nCr with the multiplication and addition principles.
nodes.quote: these techniques will be useful in determining the number of different ways of arranging and selecting objects without actually listing them
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Decision step: order or no order.
Every counting problem starts with one question: does order matter? If yes, use a permutation formula (nPr for no repetition, n^r for repetition allowed, n!/(p1!...pk!) for repeated objects). If no, use nCr. Getting this wrong is the most common error in JEE counting problems.

PART 2 — Assembly with multiplication and addition principles.
Independent successive choices are multiplied; mutually exclusive cases are added. Constraints are handled by: (a) treating a block of objects that must stay together as a single super-object, then arranging; (b) fixing special objects in their required positions first, then filling the rest; (c) subtracting the bad cases from the total (complement method).

PART 3 — Representative JEE problems.
Vowels always together in EQUATION (5 vowels, 3 consonants): treat the vowel block as 1 unit -> 4 items arranged in 4! = 24 ways, vowels within block in 5! = 120 ways -> 24 x 120 = 2880. At least 2 girls in a committee of 3 from 4 boys and 3 girls: (2 girls, 1 boy) + (3 girls, 0 boys) = 3C2 x 4C1 + 3C3 x 4C0 = 3 x 4 + 1 x 1 = 13.
keyMoves: Decide order matters (permutation) or not (combination)
Pick the right formula: nPr, n^r, n!/(p1!...pk!), or nCr
Assemble with the multiplication principle (joint choices) and addition (disjoint cases), using blocks/fixed positions/complement for constraints
misconceptions: Reaching for a formula before deciding whether order matters
Adding case counts that overlap, or multiplying counts of mutually exclusive cases
